q: New crown with food packing at the cervical embrasure — most common cause among options?
options: Too much occlusal stain characterization only
Perfect tight contact with ideal contour always
explanation: Open contacts are the classic cause of food impaction after crowns.

q: Preparation type associated with greatest pulp injury risk among listed?
explanation: Full coverage removes the most tooth structure → highest thermal/mechanical pulp risk.

q: Ferrule effect for a crowned endodontically treated tooth refers to:
options: Only the post length inside the canal without coronal tooth
Circumferential sound tooth structure height engaging the crown for fracture resistance
explanation: ≈2 mm ferrule of sound dentin improves resistance form.

q: Inadequate ferrule and short clinical crown — strategies include:
options: Crown lengthening / orthodontic extrusion / consider differently (onlay/RPD) case-based
Always place longer post only without ferrule concern
explanation: Need biologic width respect + ferrule; lengthen or rethink plan.

q: Chamfer finish line is classically used for:
options: Only all porcelain shoulder always without exception
Cast metal and many zirconia/metal-ceramic margin designs (material-dependent)
Only field occlusal rest seat on RPD
explanation: Chamfer common for metal and many monolithic zirconia margins.

q: Rounded shoulder finish line often preferred for:
options: Only Class V GI without crown
All-ceramic crowns needing bulk of ceramic at margin
explanation: Ceramics need rounded shoulder / deep chamfer for strength at margin.

q: Biological width violation by crown margin commonly results in:
options: Chronic gingival inflammation / discomfort / possible bone loss
explanation: Invading supracrestal tissue attachment → persistent inflammation.

q: Supragingival margins advantages include:
options: Easier hygiene, impression accuracy, and less tissue trauma
explanation: When esthetics allow, supragingival is periodontally friendlier.

q: Open margin on a crown clinically may show:
options: Catch with explorer, plaque trap, sensitivity, recurrent caries risk
Perfect seal always if shade is good
explanation: Open margins fail — remake often needed.

q: Retention of a crown is primarily increased by:
options: Only relying on temporary cement long-term
Taller preparation walls and limiting taper (reasonable total occlusal convergence)
Making walls as short and overly tapered as possible
explanation: Height + opposing walls + taper control = retention/resistance.

q: Total occlusal convergence ideally approximates:
options: A modest taper (often cited ~6–12° total, clinically often higher) — avoid over-taper
explanation: Over-taper kills retention; undercut prevents seating.

q: Resistance form resists:
explanation: Resistance ≠ retention; grooves/boxes help short preps.

q: Die spacer in lab work is used to:
options: Increase occlusal vertical dimension of denture
Provide cement space for complete seating of casting/crown
explanation: Die spacer ≈ cement film thickness allowance.

q: PVS (addition silicone) impression advantage:
options: Hydrophilic as water itself without technique
Excellent dimensional stability and accuracy for definitive impressions
Must pour within 30 seconds always or worthless
Cannot be used with stock trays ever
explanation: PVS: stable, accurate; still need dry field/technique.

q: Polyether impression material is noted for:
options: Good hydrophilicity / wettability but can be stiff and absorb water if stored wet
Only used for complete denture preliminary always
explanation: Polyether: hydrophilic, rigid; store dry.

q: Alginate is most appropriate for:
options: Preliminary impressions / study casts / opposing casts — not definitive crown margins usually
Border molded complete denture final only always
Definitive implant open-tray multiunit final always first choice
explanation: Irreversible hydrocolloid: prelim, not precision finals.

q: Retraction cord main purpose before impression:
options: Displace gingiva to capture finish line
explanation: Tissue management for margin capture.

q: Provisional crown important functions include:
options: Only shade practice without other roles
Pulp protection, positional stability, gingival health, and occlusal function
explanation: Temps are therapeutic, not just placeholders.

q: Post retention is less critical than:
options: Preserving radicular dentin and achieving ferrule/coronal retention of core/crown
Making the widest longest post possible always
explanation: Posts retain cores — they don't strengthen roots; ferrule matters more.

q: Easiest post type to retrieve among common options?
options: Silver point endo fill used as post
Threaded metal active post always safest and easiest
Fiber post (more than cast metal often)
explanation: Fiber posts more retrievable; cast posts harder.

q: Resin cement is often chosen for:
options: Silica-based ceramics / bonding protocols when isolation allows
explanation: Adhesive resin cements for etchable ceramics (with proper priming).

q: RMGI cement commonly used for:
options: Metal and zirconia crowns when adhesive ceramic protocol not required
Only direct pulp cap as MTA replacement always
explanation: RMGI: fluoride, easier handling; popular for many crowns.

q: Zirconia bonding challenge is mainly due to:
options: Lack of silica glass phase — needs special primers (MDP) / retention form rather than classic HF etch
Being identical to lithium disilicate HF etch protocol without change
explanation: Zirconia ≠ glass ceramic; MDP primers + mechanical retention.

q: Lithium disilicate (e.max-type) cementation often involves:
options: Only glass ionomer without any etch/silane consideration always
explanation: Glass ceramics: etch + silane adhesive pathway.

q: Cantilever FPD major risk is:
options: Always stronger than fixed-fixed long span
explanation: Cantilevers need strict case selection; forces unfavorable.

q: Minimum safety distance commonly taught between implant and inferior alveolar nerve?
explanation: ≈2 mm safety zone to IAN is a high-yield exam figure.

q: Osseointegration means:
options: Soft tissue fibrous encapsulation only as success
Only screw tightness without bone contact
Direct structural/functional connection between ordered living bone and implant surface
explanation: Brånemark concept: bone–implant interface without intervening soft tissue.

q: Peri-implant mucositis is characterized by:
options: Always advanced crater bone loss and mobility
Inflammation of soft tissues around implant without progressive crestal bone loss (beyond initial remodeling)
explanation: Mucositis = soft tissue; peri-implantitis includes bone loss.

q: Peri-implantitis includes:
options: Only cement excess without any bone change ever
Inflammation with progressive loss of supporting bone around a functioning implant
Only reversible gingivitis on natural teeth
explanation: Peri-implantitis: plaque-related bone loss around implants.

q: Uncontrolled diabetes patient needing tooth replacement — often preferred initially?
options: Immediate full-arch implants same day without control
Removable prosthesis options until systemic control improves
explanation: Poorly controlled DM → higher implant failure; reversible RPD often safer interim.

q: Smoking effect on implants?
explanation: Smoking is a major risk factor for implant complications.

q: Minimum inter-implant distance often cited for standard implants to preserve bone?
options: About 3 mm between adjacent implants (platform/concept dependent)
explanation: ≈3 mm inter-implant helps maintain interproximal bone (classic teaching).

q: Distance from implant to adjacent natural tooth often taught ≈:
explanation: ≈1.5 mm to adjacent tooth to protect periodontal bone.

q: Primary stability of an implant refers to:
options: Only soft tissue color at 1 year
Mechanical stability at placement from bone engagement
explanation: Primary = mechanical at surgery; secondary = biologic osseointegration.

q: Immediate loading requires primarily:
options: Always loading mobile implants in poor bone without splinting plan
High primary stability and appropriate occlusal scheme/case selection
No stability needed if cement is strong
explanation: ISQ/torque thresholds and prosthetic control matter for immediate load.

q: Cement-retained implant crown excess cement is associated with:
explanation: Residual cement is a classic peri-implantitis contributor.

q: Screw-retained implant crown advantage:
options: Always better esthetics in every anterior case without exception
Retrievability and avoidance of residual cement
explanation: Screw-retained: retrievable, no cement; access hole/esthetics tradeoffs.

q: Cover screw purpose during submerged healing:
options: Act as final abutment for crown
Seal implant platform during osseointegration submerged protocol
explanation: Cover screw protects internals in two-stage submerged approach.

q: Healing abutment is used to:
options: Shape soft tissue emergence during transmucosal healing
Deliver final torque only without soft tissue role
Replace need for surgical guide always
explanation: Healing collar guides gingival architecture.

q: Platform switching concept aims to:
options: Widen every abutment to bone edge always
Medialize implant–abutment junction to reduce crestal bone remodeling stress/inflammation horizontally
explanation: Platform switch: narrower abutment on wider platform — biologic width horizontal component.

q: CBCT in implant planning is valuable for:
options: Only caries diagnosis on smooth surfaces
3D bone volume, nerve canal, sinus anatomy assessment
explanation: Cross-sectional imaging for safe implant placement.

q: Sinus lift is considered when:
options: Always for every single tooth implant
Mandibular IAN is too close only
Posterior maxilla residual bone height insufficient for desired implant length
explanation: Augment sinus floor when RBH inadequate.

q: Implant mobility after supposed integration period suggests:
options: Normal healthy implant always mobile like tooth PDL
Failure of osseointegration / mechanical failure until proven otherwise
explanation: Integrated implants should not be mobile.

q: Keratinized mucosa around implants — clinical relevance often taught:
options: Only mobile mucosa always ideal for hygiene
Soft tissue type never matters around implants
Adequate keratinized tissue may facilitate hygiene and comfort (debated quantity)
explanation: Many clinicians prefer ≥2 mm KT for maintainability.

q: Antibiotic prophylaxis for routine implant placement in healthy patient?
options: Practice varies; not a substitute for sterile technique — follow current evidence/protocol
Megadose forever without indication always mandatory identical to IE high-risk always
Never consider infection risk at all
explanation: Evidence mixed for routine Abx; asepsis primary; high-risk patients individualized.

q: Kennedy Class I is:
options: Unilateral edentulous area with natural teeth both sides
Anterior edentulous area crossing midline only
explanation: Class I = bilateral free-end saddles.

q: Kennedy Class II is:
explanation: Class II = one side free-end.

q: Kennedy Class III is:
options: Unilateral edentulous area with natural teeth remaining both anterior and posterior to it
explanation: Class III = tooth-bounded unilateral space.

q: Kennedy Class IV is:
options: Single anterior edentulous area that crosses the midline
explanation: Class IV = anterior crossing midline; no modifications by definition.

q: Applegate rule: classification should be done:
options: After extractions planned are done and with most posterior edentulous area determining class
Only by number of missing anteriors always ignoring posteriors
explanation: Applegate rules refine Kennedy application.

q: Most destructive force direction on RPD abutments?
options: Forces only toward residual ridge mucosa always harmless
Light vertical along long axis of abutment
explanation: Lateral torquing forces damage abutments most.

q: Distal extension RPD support is derived from:
options: Combination of residual ridge mucosa and abutment teeth
Teeth only with zero ridge contact ideal
explanation: Tooth-tissue borne for Class I/II.

q: Occlusal rest seat preparation should ideally direct forces:
options: Along the long axis of the abutment tooth
explanation: Rests transmit vertical forces axially.

q: Lingual bar major connector requires approximately:
options: Only 1 mm total height always enough
Zero clearance from gingiva always touching
Placement on sloping unattached tissue without depth
Adequate depth from free gingival margin to floor of mouth (often ≥7–8 mm functional depth concept)
explanation: Insufficient depth → use lingual plate instead.

q: Lingual plate is preferred when:
options: Always when depth is huge and teeth perfect only reason
Insufficient lingual vestibular depth for a bar / need for indirect retention / future tooth loss anticipated
explanation: Plate when bar won't fit or added functions needed.

q: Circumferential (Akers) clasp assembly needs:
options: Rest, retentive arm, reciprocal arm, and proper encirclement (≥180°)
Only a retentive tip without rest
explanation: Clasp components: support, retention, reciprocation, encirclement, passivity.

q: RPI clasp system components are:
options: Only wrought wire on all teeth without rests
Mesial rest, distal proximal plate, I-bar retentive arm
explanation: RPI: stress-releasing design for distal extensions.

q: I-bar tip engages undercut:
options: From occlusal approach only like cast circumferential always
In the gingival third from midfacial approaching from gingiva
On the lingual above height of contour only always
explanation: Infrabulge I-bar approaches from below height of contour.

q: Indirect retainer purpose on distal extension RPD:
options: Resist rotational displacement of extension base away from tissues (sticky foods)
Provide primary retention instead of clasps always
explanation: Indirect retainers (rests far from fulcrum) oppose lifting of distal base.

q: Surveying an RPD cast is done to:
options: Determine path of insertion, undercuts, and guide planes
explanation: Surveyor is essential for framework design.

q: Guide planes on abutments help:
options: Define path of insertion and enhance stability/reciprocation
Eliminate all undercuts for retention forever
explanation: Parallel guide planes improve path control.

q: Combination syndrome classic features associated with:
options: Maxillary complete denture opposing mandibular anterior teeth only (with missing posteriors) — flabby anterior maxilla, etc.
Only Class III RPD without denture
explanation: Kelly combination syndrome: CD vs lower anteriors.

q: Altered cast technique is used mainly for:
options: Improving support of distal extension bases by capturing ridge under controlled impression
explanation: Altered cast: correct tissue detail for free-end saddles.

q: Rest seat in enamel should be:
options: Saucerized rounded floor with positive seat — not sharp stress points
explanation: Proper rest geometry protects tooth and framework.

q: Wrought wire clasp advantage:
options: Greater flexibility / adjustability; kinder to periodontium in some cases
explanation: WW clasps more flexible than cast half-round often.

q: Centric relation in complete dentures is used as:
options: Only vertical dimension of rest identical always
A repeatable reference jaw relation for setting occlusion when ICP not available
The shade of artificial teeth only
explanation: CR is a bone-to-bone relation independent of teeth.

q: Vertical dimension of occlusion (VDO) if excessively increased causes:
options: Clicking teeth, facial strain, sore ridges, difficulty speaking/closing
explanation: Over-opened VDO → fatigue, sore spots, phonetics issues.

q: Excessively decreased VDO may cause:
options: Only increased freeway space never a problem
Overclosure appearance, angular cheilitis risk, reduced chewing efficiency
explanation: Collapsed VDO: aged look, possible cheilitis, poor function.

q: Freeway space (interocclusal rest space) is approximately often cited:
options: About 2–4 mm between rest position and VDO (individual variation)
explanation: VDR − VDO ≈ freeway space.

q: Primary stress-bearing area in mandibular complete denture often includes:
options: Retromolar pad only without shelf role
Only sharp mylohyoid ridge without relief
explanation: Buccal shelf is cortical and good support zone.

q: Maxillary primary stress-bearing areas include:
options: Soft palate vibrating line only as sole support
Hard palate residual ridge (horizontal portions) — with relief of incisive papilla/raphe as needed
explanation: Support from firm palate and ridges; relieve midline raphe/torus as needed.

q: Posterior palatal seal (post dam) purpose:
options: Compensate acrylic shrinkage and maintain peripheral seal at junction of hard/soft palate
explanation: PPS enhances retention via seal.

q: Vibrating line helps locate:
options: Posterior limit of maxillary denture / PPS area
Occlusal plane of natural Class I only
explanation: Ah line / vibrating line for posterior extension.

q: Border molding captures:
options: Functional depth and width of vestibules for peripheral seal
explanation: Custom tray borders molded to muscle function.

q: Common cause of generalized sore mouth under new denture?
options: Occlusal errors / excessive VDO / poor adaptation — diagnose systematically
Always only vitamin deficiency without mechanical check
Ignore and reline with household glue
explanation: Rule out occlusion, extension, adaptation before systemic-only thinking.

q: Sore spot on sharp mylohyoid ridge — management often:
options: Extract the ridge with elevator without planning
Add more acrylic bulk always on sharp ridge
Selective relief of denture base over ridge + ensure occlusion correct
explanation: Relief over undercut/sharp anatomy; surgery only if needed.

q: Balanced occlusion in complete dentures aims to:
options: Eliminate all posterior contact in centric
Contact only on one side always in all excursions
Only anterior contact in all movements
Provide multi-tooth contacts in eccentric excursions to stabilize bases
explanation: Balance helps stability of mucosa-borne bases.

q: Immediate denture advantage:
options: Perfect fit forever without reline ever
Patient never without teeth; soft tissue support/esthetics after extractions
Always better than conventional without tradeoffs
explanation: Immediate CD: esthetics/psychologic benefit; needs relines.

q: Tissue conditioner is used to:
options: Temporarily improve abused tissue health under ill-fitting denture before final impressions
Permanently replace hard reline forever always
explanation: Soft tissue conditioners for abused mucosa — short-term.

q: Neutral zone technique records:
options: Potential denture space where soft tissue forces are balanced for tooth positioning
explanation: Neutral zone helps stability especially in resorbed mandibles.

q: Microfilled composites are known for:
options: Highest strength for all posterior stress always
High polishability but relatively lower strength/fracture resistance vs hybrids
Only used as amalgam substitute in high load without limits
explanation: Microfill: esthetic polish; weaker — hybrid/nanohybrid for stress.

q: Fractured microfilled anterior restoration needing strength — better replacement?
options: Another pure microfill only for max strength
explanation: Upgrade to hybrid/nanohybrid for mechanical properties.

q: Amalgam advantage includes:
options: Durability, wear resistance, and technique tolerance in wet fields relatively
Bonds micromechanically to enamel like etched composite without any prep retention
explanation: Amalgam: tough, but esthetics/prep retention form needed.

q: Gamma-2 phase in low-copper amalgam is associated with:
options: Corrosion and weakened properties — reduced in high-copper amalgams
explanation: High-copper amalgams eliminate much γ2 (Sn-Hg).

q: Creep in amalgam refers to:
options: Only setting expansion in first 3 seconds
Time-dependent plastic deformation under load — less in high-copper alloys
explanation: Creep related to marginal breakdown historically.

q: Glass ionomer cement bonds by:
options: Only micromechanical etch-and-rinse identical to all composites without ion exchange
Chemical adhesion (ionic) to tooth structure and releases fluoride
explanation: GI: polyacid-hydroxyapatite interaction + F release.

q: ZOE cement common use:
options: Direct permanent pulp capping gold standard over MTA always
Temporary cementation / provisional sedative restorations (eugenol caveats with resin)
Definitive cement for all silica ceramics always first choice
explanation: ZOE: temporaries; eugenol can inhibit some resin polymerization.

q: Calcium hydroxide traditional role:
options: Definitive core buildup under high stress alone always
Pulp capping liner with high pH antibacterial effect (modern MTA often preferred)
explanation: Ca(OH)2: classic pulp liner; solubility issues under restorations.

q: MTA / bioceramic advantages for pulp and perforation repair include:
options: Sealing ability, biocompatibility, moisture tolerance relative to older materials
Tooth color perfect always for all formulations
Fast 5-second set always for all products without exception
Cheaper than Ca(OH)2 always only advantage
explanation: MTA: excellent seal; long set (classic); possible discoloration.

q: EDTA in endodontics primarily:
options: Dissolves organic pulp tissue better than NaOCl
Chelates and removes inorganic smear layer
explanation: NaOCl = organic; EDTA = inorganic smear.

q: Sodium hypochlorite endodontic role:
options: Dissolve organic tissue and disinfect canal system
Remove only smear inorganic exclusively better than EDTA
explanation: NaOCl is primary irrigant for organic dissolution/disinfection.

q: Mixing CHX and NaOCl can produce:
options: A more effective clear solution always desirable
Only oxygen beneficial foam without precipitate risk
Precipitate (PCA-related brown/orange) — avoid mixing in canal
explanation: Don't mix CHX + NaOCl; intermediate rinse.

q: Hybrid layer in adhesive dentistry is:
options: Only smear layer left intact without resin
explanation: Hybrid layer = micromechanical adhesion basis on dentin.

q: Total-etch (etch-and-rinse) on enamel aims to:
options: Smear intact bonding without etch always
Only desiccate without rinsing acids ever
Create microporosities for resin micromechanical retention
explanation: Phosphoric acid etch enamel classic pattern.

q: Over-drying dentin after etch in etch-and-rinse systems can cause:
options: Collapse of collagen network and reduced bond quality
explanation: Moist bonding concept — don't desiccate etched dentin.

q: C-factor (configuration factor) is:
options: Only film thickness of cement in mm always 1:1
Ratio of bonded to unbonded surfaces — high C-factor increases shrinkage stress
explanation: Class I box has high C-factor → incremental fill strategies.

q: Polymerization shrinkage of composite clinically may cause:
options: Gap formation, sensitivity, white line margins, cuspal deflection
Expansion that always closes gaps beneficially only
explanation: Manage with increments, low-shrink materials, soft-start concepts.

q: Radiopacity of restorative materials is important to:
options: Detect excess material, voids, and recurrent caries on radiographs
explanation: ISO radiopacity requirements aid diagnosis.

q: Addition silicone (PVS) inhibition of set can occur with:
options: Sulfur from latex gloves contaminating the putty/wash
Only water making it set faster always beneficial
Only eugenol making set perfect always
explanation: Latex sulfur poisons PVS platinum catalyst — use vinyl gloves.

q: Coefficient of thermal expansion mismatch between material and tooth can lead to:
options: Marginal percolation / gap stress with temperature cycling
Perfect eternal seal always if CTE differs a lot
explanation: CTE compatibility reduces microleakage risk.
